content:
# Sets

Unordered collection of items.

Every set element is

-   Unique (no duplicates)
-   Must be immutable

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/fee4f127-5199-4baf-9371-bf686d47f158.png)

## Creating a Set

- Created by enclosing elements within *{curly}* brackets.
- Each item is separated by a comma.

#### Code

```python
a = 2
set_a = {5, "Six", a, 8.2}
print(type(set_a))
print(set_a)
```


#### Output

```
<class 'set'>
{8.2, 2, 'Six', 5}
```

Need not be in the same order as defined.


## No Duplicate Items

Sets contain unique elements

#### Code

```python
set_a = {"a", "b", "c", "a"}
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

{'b', 'a', 'c'}
```

## Immutable Items

Set items must be immutable.
As List is mutable, Set cannot have list as an item.

#### Code

```python
set_a = {"a", ["c", "a"]}
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
```

## Creating Empty Set

We use `set()` to create an empty set.

#### Code

```python
set_a = set()
print(type(set_a))
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```
<class 'set'>
set()
```

## Converting to Set

`set(sequence)` takes any sequence as argument and converts to set, avoiding duplicates 

### List to Set


#### Code

```python
set_a = set([1,2,1])
print(type(set_a))
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```
<class 'set'>
{1, 2}
```

### String to Set

#### Code

```python
set_a = set("apple")
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

{'l', 'p', 'e', 'a'}
```

### Tuple to Set


#### Code

```python
set_a = set((1, 2, 1))
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

{1, 2}
```

## Accessing Items

As sets are unordered, we cannot access or change an item of a set using 

- Indexing
- Slicing

#### Code

```python
set_a = {1, 2, 3}
print(set_a[1])
print(set_a[1:3])
```

#### Output

```

TypeError: 'set' object is not subscriptable
```

## Adding Items

`set.add(value)` adds the item to the set, if the item is not present already.

#### Code

```python
set_a = {1, 3, 6, 2, 9}
set_a.add(7)
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

{1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 9}
```

## Adding Multiple Items
`set.update(sequence)` adds multiple items to the set, and duplicates are avoided.

#### Code

```python
set_a = {1, 3, 9}
set_a.update([2, 3])
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

{2, 1, 3, 9}
```

## Removing Specific Item

`set.discard(value)` takes a single value and removes if present.

#### Code

```python
set_a = {1, 3, 9}
set_a.discard(3)
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```

{1, 9}
```

`set_a.remove(value)` takes a value and remove if it present or raise an error.

#### Code

```python
set_a = {1, 3, 9}
set_a.remove(5)
print(set_a)
```

#### Output

```
KeyError: 5
```

## Operations on Sets

You can perform the following operations on Sets

- `clear()`
- `len()`
- Iterating
- Membership Check
